channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Why redesigning workflows around AI matters more than the tool itself #ai #work
executive_summary: Benedict Evans presented a 90-slide talk in Singapore highlighting the strategic shifts in AI adoption. He emphasized that AI follows familiar platform cycles and that teams must redesign workflows around AI to stay competitive. Evans warned that those treating AI as optional will fall behind those who integrate it into their organizational structure and strategies.
bullets: Benedict Evans argues that AI adoption follows familiar platform cycles, similar to how smartphones evolved. This means that just as smartphones transformed communication and business, AI will reshape industries in predictable phases. Think of it like the evolution of transportation: first came the horse, then the car, and now we are seeing the rise of self-driving vehicles.

Large language models are becoming essential inputs in AI workflows, while frontier labs maintain their unique advantages. This means that the best teams will leverage these models to enhance their processes, while those without access will struggle. It's like using a high-quality tool in carpentry; the right tool can make all the difference in the final product.

Path-dependent adoption refers to how organizations evolve based on their past decisions regarding technology. This means that companies that have already invested in certain tools will find it harder to switch to new AI-driven workflows. Think of it like a train on a track; once it's set on a path, changing direction requires significant effort.

AI agents are reshaping roles and coordination within organizations, leading to enhanced leverage at the organizational level. This means that AI can take over repetitive tasks, allowing humans to focus on more strategic work. It's like having a personal assistant who handles your scheduling, freeing you up to focus on important meetings.

Teams that treat AI as optional tooling will lose ground to those redesigning workflows around its inevitability. This means that organizations must integrate AI into their core strategies rather than viewing it as an add-on. Imagine trying to bake a cake without the right ingredients; you might end up with a mess instead of a delicious dessert.

Evans emphasizes that organizations must rethink their vendor strategies in light of AI's rapid evolution. This means that companies should not just stick with their current suppliers but explore new partnerships that align with AI advancements. It's like a chef experimenting with new spices to elevate a dish; sticking to the same old flavors can lead to bland results.

The integration of AI into workflows is not just about technology but also about cultural shifts within organizations. This means that teams must foster an environment that embraces change and innovation. Think of it like a sports team; if players are resistant to new strategies, they will struggle to win games.

AI's impact on organizational structures means that traditional hierarchies may need to be flattened. This allows for quicker decision-making and more agile responses to market changes. It's like a startup where everyone wears multiple hats; flexibility can lead to faster growth.

Evans suggests that organizations should prioritize training their teams on AI tools and workflows. This means investing in education to ensure that employees are equipped to leverage AI effectively. Think of it like teaching someone to swim; without proper training, they might struggle in the water.

The timeline for significant AI adoption is accelerating, with Evans indicating that organizations must act within the next 4-5 months to stay competitive. This means that waiting too long could result in missed opportunities. It's like a race; the sooner you start running, the better your chances of winning.

Evans highlights that 80% of AI implementation should focus on functional aspects, while only 20% should be on hygiene factors. This means that teams must prioritize effective use of AI over minor details that don't impact performance. Think of it like building a house; focusing on a strong foundation is crucial, while paint color can wait.

The concept of organizational leverage through AI means that teams can achieve more with fewer resources. This allows for increased productivity and efficiency across the board. It's like using a lever to lift a heavy object; with the right tools, you can accomplish tasks that would otherwise be impossible.
key_takeaways: Redesign your workflows to integrate AI tools within the next 4-5 months. This is crucial because those who wait will fall behind competitors who adapt quickly. If you skip this step, you risk becoming obsolete in a rapidly evolving market.

Ensure that at least 80% of your AI implementation focuses on functional capabilities, not just hygiene factors. This matters because prioritizing performance will lead to better outcomes and efficiency. If you neglect this, you may end up with a system that looks good but doesn't work effectively.

Invest in training your team on AI tools and workflows immediately. This is important because a well-trained team can leverage AI to its fullest potential, driving innovation and productivity. If you don't act now, your team may struggle to keep up with the competition.

technical_terms.large language models: These are advanced AI systems designed to understand and generate human language. Think of it like a translator who not only knows the words but also understands the context and nuances of conversation.
technical_terms.path-dependent adoption: This refers to how past decisions influence future technology choices within organizations. Like a river carving its path through the landscape, past choices shape the flow of future developments.
technical_terms.AI agents: These are software programs that can perform tasks autonomously using AI. Think of them like virtual assistants that can manage your calendar, answer emails, and even make reservations without needing constant human input.
technical_terms.workflow redesign: This is the process of changing how tasks are organized and executed within an organization to better integrate new technologies. Like rearranging furniture in a room to create a more functional space, it can lead to improved efficiency and productivity.
technical_terms.organizational leverage: This refers to the ability of an organization to achieve greater results with fewer resources through effective use of technology. Think of it like a seesaw; with the right balance, you can lift heavier weights with less effort.
technical_terms.vendor strategy: This is the plan an organization has for selecting and managing its suppliers and partners. Like choosing the right ingredients for a recipe, the right vendors can make or break the success of a project.
technical_terms.platform cycles: These are the predictable phases through which technology platforms evolve over time. Think of it like the life cycle of a product; it goes through introduction, growth, maturity, and decline.
technical_terms.cultural shifts: These are changes in the values and behaviors of an organization that can impact how technology is adopted. Like a school adopting a new teaching method, cultural shifts can lead to better engagement and outcomes.
technical_terms.functional capabilities: These are the practical abilities of a system or tool to perform its intended tasks effectively. Think of it like the engine of a car; if it runs well, the car can take you where you need to go.
technical_terms.hygiene factors: These are the minor details that ensure a system runs smoothly but do not directly impact its core functionality. Like keeping your car clean; while it looks nice, it doesn't affect how well it drives.
